summary:
President Suharto of Indonesia, who had ruled the country for thirty-two years after seizing power in 1967, resigned on May 21, 1998, brought down by the Asian financial crisis and massive popular protests. The crisis hit Indonesia with particular severity: the Indonesian rupiah lost over eighty percent of its value against the US dollar, unemployment surged, and food prices soared. The IMF's structural adjustment conditions — cutting fuel and food subsidies — added further economic pain.

Mass demonstrations led by students spread across the country in May 1998. Violent riots in Jakarta, in which Chinese-Indonesian businesses were targeted and hundreds of people killed, alarmed military commanders and business elites. When key military figures and members of his own cabinet withdrew their support, Suharto resigned and handed power to Vice President B.J. Habibie. Suharto's three decades of rule, known as the New Order, had delivered significant economic growth alongside massive corruption, brutal suppression of political opposition, and the East Timor occupation. His fall inaugurated a period of democratic reformation (Reformasi) that transformed Indonesia into one of the world's largest democracies, though the transition involved continuing political instability and the eventual independence of East Timor in 2002.
